pattern: Greedy target-enumeration: when op2 is cheaper, raise the target to maximize pairing opportunities, being careful to account for the constraint that the hardest element cannot be paired with itself.
hints: If 2*cost1 <= cost2, op2 is never beneficial; just use op1 for all deficits: answer = (max - min) * n... wait, total deficit to reach current max, computed as sum(max - nums[i]).

Otherwise, enumerate candidate target values from max(nums) up to max(nums, sum - 2*min) + 1. At each target, the deficit of the globally-minimum element (max_deficit) may exceed the combined deficits of all other elements (rest = total - max_deficit). In that case you must use op1 for the excess and pair the rest with max_deficit via op2.
The formula per target: if max_deficit > rest, cost = rest * cost2 + (max_deficit - rest) * cost1; else cost = (total // 2) * cost2 + (total % 2) * cost1. Take the minimum over all candidate targets.

edge_inputs.description: two elements op1 only because cost2 useless
op1 cheaper per unit despite imbalance
need to raise target beyond max to maximize pairing
op2 is cheap but answer requires target beyond initial max

stress.description: random small arrays with varied cost ratios
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 20) for _ in range(n)]
    c1 = rng.randint(1, 10)
    c2 = rng.randint(1, 10)
    return [nums, c1, c2]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minCostToEqualizeArray(self, nums, cost1: int, cost2: int) -> int:
        MOD = 10**9 + 7
        n = len(nums)
        if n == 1:
            return 0
        mx = max(nums)
        mn = min(nums)
        s = sum(nums)
        best = float('inf')
        # Try all targets in a generous range
        for target in range(mx, max(mx, s - 2*mn) + 2):
            total = target * n - s
            max_d = target - mn
            rest = total - max_d
            if max_d > rest:
                max_pairs = rest
            else:
                max_pairs = total // 2
            # For each number of pairs, cost = p*cost2 + (total-2p)*cost1
            # Minimized at max_pairs (since 2*cost1 > cost2 when op2 worth using)
            # But if 2*cost1 <= cost2 don't use op2 at all
            if 2 * cost1 <= cost2:
                cost = total * cost1
            else:
                p = max_pairs
                cost = p * cost2 + (total - 2*p) * cost1
            best = min(best, cost)
        return best % MOD
